title: SEO Config Toolbox — Migrating an SSoT to a Database (ADR)
description: An architecture-decision walkthrough based on a real proposal: migrate the Single Source of Truth for SEO context configurations from code-embedded CSV resources and hardcoded values to a managed document database (AWS DocumentDB), so SEO experts can edit configs through a self-service Toolbox instead of filing engineering tickets. The governing constraint is CQRS-lite: completely redefine the write path while leaving the ultra-fast in-memory read path entirely untouched.

nodes.label: SEO Expert
nodes.description: Domain expert who edits SEO context configurations; previously had to ask engineers.

nodes.label: SEO Toolbox UI
nodes.description: Self-service web UI; lets SEO experts edit contexts, clusters, and text config directly.

nodes.label: SEO Toolbox API
nodes.description: Backend CRUD API for the Toolbox: validates input, enforces OCC, writes the audit log.

nodes.label: Company IdP (RBAC)
nodes.description: Corporate identity provider; governs Toolbox access via role-based access control.

nodes.label: AWS DocumentDB 5.0
nodes.description: Multi-AZ document store holding seo_contexts, clusters, version pointer, and audit log. The new write-side SSoT.

nodes.label: AWS Secrets Manager
nodes.description: Holds rotating DocumentDB credentials, injected into ECS tasks; 30-day dual-secret rotation.

nodes.label: Embedded CSV Resources
nodes.description: SEO config CSVs compiled into the application assembly (Data/SLB/); the legacy write surface.

nodes.label: Hardcoded Config Values
nodes.description: Base contexts, WhiteLabel URLs, and search queries hardcoded in the codebase.

nodes.label: Daily Batch Job
nodes.description: ECS scheduled task that runs once a day, compiles the full SEO payload, and bumps the version pointer.

nodes.label: SeoConfigLoader (code)
nodes.description: Legacy loader: parses embedded CSVs + hardcoded values via CsvHelper streaming.

nodes.label: DocumentDbSeoConfigLoader
nodes.description: New loader: queries all published docs from DocumentDB in parallel and maps them to existing DTOs.

nodes.label: SeoConfigComparer
nodes.description: Shadow-mode validator: structurally diffs code-loader vs DocumentDB-loader output (set-equality on lists).

nodes.label: Data Lake (CSV in RAM)
nodes.description: Large CSV loaded entirely into RAM for ID mapping during enrichment.

nodes.label: External GEO APIs
nodes.description: External geo services used to enrich contexts.

nodes.label: DynamoDB (GEO relations)
nodes.description: Geo-relations lookup used during enrichment.

nodes.label: PostgreSQL (Classifieds)
nodes.description: Classifieds data joined in during enrichment.

nodes.label: SeoConfigRefresher
nodes.description: Background service in the API that polls the version pointer every 5 min and atomically swaps the cache.

nodes.label: SeoConfigSnapshot (Cache)
nodes.description: In-memory FrozenDictionary giving O(1) lookups; zero network I/O on the request hot path.

nodes.label: Application API Layer
nodes.description: Serves millions of client read requests from the in-memory snapshot.

nodes.label: Client Read Request
nodes.description: End-user / frontend request served from the cache; never reaches DocumentDB.

nodes.label: Datadog / CloudWatch
nodes.description: APM, discrepancy logs, audit-failure alerts, and batch-failure alerting.

capacityDiagram: Writes[Writes: a handful of SEO experts]

functional: Let SEO experts create, edit, publish, and archive SEO context configurations through a UI, without a code change or deployment.
Manage LinkBox clusters (groupings of contexts) and per-context text config, WhiteLabel URLs, and search queries.
Validate input on the write path so a bad SEO parameter can never crash the daily batch.
Record a granular audit trail: who changed which field, from what value to what value, and when.
Keep serving the live application from the daily-compiled config exactly as before (parity).
nonFunctional: Protect the read path at all costs: client API requests stay zero-I/O O(1) in-memory lookups; the database is never on the hot path.
Prevent silent lost updates when two experts edit the same context concurrently.
Reversible, zero-downtime migration with mathematically proven payload parity before cutover.
Security: private-subnet-only DB, encryption at rest/in transit, rotating credentials, RBAC, point-in-time recovery.
Accepted constraint: config saved in the Toolbox propagates to live traffic only at the next daily batch (up to 24h) in v1.

capacity.label: Write actors
capacity.note: SEO experts editing admin config — not a high-volume write workload

capacity.label: Read volume
capacity.note: served entirely from the in-memory FrozenDictionary

capacity.label: Propagation SLA
capacity.note: Toolbox save → next daily batch → cache swap (v1)

capacity.label: Refresher poll
capacity.note: background poll of the version pointer; atomic cache swap on change

capacity.label: Missing estimates
capacity.note: doc count, published-doc size, batch read throughput — needed to justify instance sizing

api.description: Load a single SEO context for editing; returns the current OCC version.
Save edits with optimistic concurrency control. Validates schema; inserts an audit record (best-effort).
Transition draft → published (or unpublish with explicit confirmation).
Save a LinkBox cluster; writes currentContextIds and targetedContextIds together.

dataModel.name: seo_contexts
dataModel.note: Core collection. Same-lifecycle data (text config, WL URL, search query) embedded. Indexes: {brandId,status}, {brandId,contextId} unique.

dataModel.name: seo_link_box_clusters
dataModel.note: Cluster groupings referencing contexts by numeric id (avoids duplication). Index: {brandId,clusterCode} unique.

dataModel.name: seo_config_metadata
dataModel.note: Single-document version pointer for cache invalidation. Bumped ONLY by the daily batch.

dataModel.name: seo_audit_log
dataModel.note: Append-only. TTL index purges >90 days. Index: {brandId,documentId,modifiedAt}.

patterns.name: CQRS-lite
patterns.what: Separate the write surface (Toolbox → DocumentDB) from the read surface (in-memory cache). Already present; this migration formalizes the write side.
patterns.whenToUse: Read-dominated systems where the read path must stay fast and the write path needs richer validation/governance.

patterns.name: Optimistic Concurrency Control
patterns.what: Carry a version on each document; write only if the version still matches, else 409.
patterns.whenToUse: Multiple users can edit the same record and silent last-write-wins is unacceptable.

patterns.name: Version-pointer cache invalidation
patterns.what: A single metadata doc holds a version; a background poller swaps the in-memory snapshot atomically when it changes.
patterns.whenToUse: You want a hot in-memory cache refreshed off a slow write store without putting the store on the hot path.

patterns.name: Shadow mode / parity validation
patterns.what: Run old and new code paths in parallel, structurally diff their outputs, and require zero discrepancies before cutover.
patterns.whenToUse: Migrating a data source where output must be provably identical.

patterns.name: Best-effort side-write
patterns.what: Commit the primary write, then attempt the secondary (audit) write; on failure, alert but don't fail the request.
patterns.whenToUse: A secondary record is valuable but not worth multi-document transaction overhead.

steps.title: 1. Naive: Config Lives in the Code (the baseline)
description: The system already works: SEO config is compiled into the application — embedded CSV resources (Data/SLB/) plus hardcoded values. A once-a-day ECS batch (SeoConfigLoader) parses them with CsvHelper, enriches with the Data Lake, GEO APIs, DynamoDB, and PostgreSQL, and produces a compiled payload. The API serves millions of client requests from an in-memory FrozenDictionary snapshot — O(1), zero network I/O.

The read path is excellent and we must not touch it. The problem is entirely on the write path: every SEO change is a code change → pull request → deployment, with an engineer as a mandatory data-entry intermediary (an estimated 20–30% of squad capacity per sprint).

It has no concurrency control (two experts can't safely edit), no input validation (a bad parameter crashes the batch), and no audit trail (no record of who changed what, when, or why). These are the gaps the next steps close — without disturbing the read path.

view.caption: As-Is: config is compiled into the app; a daily batch builds the payload; the API serves reads from an in-memory cache.

steps.decisionPrompt: The read path is great. What's actually broken, and where must any fix go?
whyNow: Naming the baseline pins the one rule that governs every later decision: redefine the write path, protect the read path.

traps.trap: Treating this as a read-path / performance problem.
traps.why: The in-memory FrozenDictionary read path is already optimal; rebuilding it adds risk for no gain.
traps.instead: Scope the work strictly to the write path; make 'don't touch the read path' an explicit constraint.

traps.trap: Shipping 'engineers as data-entry intermediaries' as good enough.
traps.why: It silently burns 20–30% of sprint capacity and gates every SEO experiment on a deploy cycle.
traps.instead: Introduce a database SSoT so a self-service Toolbox becomes technically possible.

recap.before: Config in code; engineers gate every change.
recap.after: We see the read path is fine and the write path is the whole problem.
recap.newRisk: Self-service is impossible without a real write-side store — so introduce one.

steps.title: 2. Introduce a Database as the Write-Side SSoT
description: Add a managed document database (AWS DocumentDB 5.0) as the new write-side Single Source of Truth, and a Toolbox (UI + API) on top of it. SEO experts edit through the UI; the API validates and writes to DocumentDB. This is the strict prerequisite that makes a self-service Toolbox possible.

Crucially, DocumentDB is NEVER on the client request hot path. We are extending the existing CQRS-lite split: the write path becomes transactional and database-backed; the read path stays the untouched in-memory cache.

Why DocumentDB over the company defaults? The data is deeply nested (searchQuery params, openedLevels arrays) so a relational JSONB store fights the POCO models; and because the Toolbox UI's query/filter/sort patterns are new and will evolve fast, DynamoDB's upfront-access-pattern rigidity is a poor fit. (Note the reviewer's fair pushback: at this tiny write volume DynamoDB's Scan/PartiQL would be fine and its conditional writes are a clean OCC primitive — so OCC is not a true differentiator. The honest deciding factor is flexible ad-hoc querying for an evolving admin UI.)

view.caption: A new write surface (Toolbox UI/API → DocumentDB) sits beside the untouched read path.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why a document DB here, and how do you keep it off the read hot path?

concepts.definition: The one authoritative place a piece of data is written and owned.
concepts.whyItMatters: Moving the SSoT out of the codebase is what lets non-engineers change config safely.
concepts.example: seo_contexts in DocumentDB replaces embedded CSVs as the authoritative write store.

concepts.definition: Command (write) and query (read) use different models/stores, without full event-sourcing machinery.
concepts.whyItMatters: Lets the write side gain validation and governance while the read side stays a hot in-memory cache.
concepts.example: Writes go to DocumentDB; reads stay on the FrozenDictionary snapshot.

whyNow: Everything else (validation, OCC, audit, Toolbox) requires a real write-side store first.

recap.after: DocumentDB is the write SSoT; a Toolbox can be built on it.
recap.newRisk: The cache is still fed from code — the new store isn't wired to the read path yet.

steps.title: 3. Bridge the Stores Without Touching the Read Path
description: Repoint the daily batch and the API's background refresher at DocumentDB. A new DocumentDbSeoConfigLoader queries all published documents in parallel (Task.WhenAll) and maps them back into the existing DTOs, so downstream projectors are untouched.

Cache invalidation uses a version pointer: a single seo_config_metadata document holds a version timestamp, bumped ONLY by the daily batch on a successful build (not by Toolbox saves — that protects the 24h SLA and stops mid-day hot-reloads). The SeoConfigRefresher polls that pointer every 5 minutes; on a change it loads the new payload and calls SeoConfigSnapshot.Replace() — a single atomic swap, with the old cache fully live until the instant of replacement.
A feature flag SeoConfigSource: code | shadow | docdb governs the source, so the cutover is reversible. DocumentDB polling only activates in docdb mode.

view.caption: Proposed: the batch + refresher source from DocumentDB; the read-path cache is fed the same way as before.

steps.decisionPrompt: How does a Toolbox edit reach live traffic, and why does the batch (not the Toolbox) bump the version?

concepts.definition: A tiny document whose version field changes when the dataset changes; pollers compare it to what they have loaded.
concepts.whyItMatters: Lets a hot cache detect 'new data exists' without scanning the whole store.

concepts.definition: Build the new payload off to the side, then flip a single volatile reference.
concepts.whyItMatters: Readers never see a half-built cache; no locking on the hot path.

whyNow: A write store is useless to the live app until its data can reach the read cache safely.

recap.after: Cache fed from DocumentDB via version-pointer polling; read path unchanged.
recap.newRisk: Multiple experts can now hit the same document — concurrent edits can silently overwrite each other.

steps.title: 4. Make Concurrent Edits Safe (OCC)
description: The Toolbox is the first time multiple SEO experts can edit the same context at once. Without protection, the lost-update problem appears: A and B both open a context; B saves last and silently clobbers A's unseen changes.

Solve it with optimistic concurrency control. Each primary document carries a version integer. The UI reads the version on load. The save executes a conditional atomic update — UpdateOne({ _id, version: N }, { $set: {...}, $inc: { version: 1 } }). If another save already bumped the version, the condition matches zero documents (ModifiedCount == 0) and the API returns 409 Conflict, prompting a refresh.
(OCC could equally be done with DynamoDB conditional writes — it's not unique to DocumentDB.)

view.caption: Write path zoomed in: the API guards every save with a version-conditional update.

steps.decisionPrompt: Two experts open the same context. How do you guarantee neither silently loses their edit?

concepts.definition: Two readers write back to the same record; the later write overwrites the earlier without seeing it.
concepts.whyItMatters: It's silent data loss — the first editor's work vanishes with no error.
concepts.example: A and B both edit a title; B saves last; A's change is gone.

concepts.definition: Don't lock; carry a version and reject a write whose version no longer matches.
concepts.whyItMatters: Cheap and contention-free for low write volume; surfaces conflicts instead of hiding them.

whyNow: Concurrent editing is a brand-new capability the Toolbox introduces; it's unsafe until OCC is in place.

traps.trap: Relying on last-write-wins because 'the DB serializes writes anyway'.
traps.why: DB-level write atomicity doesn't stop a stale full-document overwrite — the lost update happens at the UI level.
traps.instead: Carry a version and make the save conditional on it; return 409 on mismatch.

recap.before: Concurrent saves silently clobber each other.
recap.after: Stale saves get a 409 and re-fetch; no silent loss.
recap.newRisk: Embedding everything in one document would still let one big edit lock/duplicate data — model the collections carefully.

steps.title: 5. Model the Data: Normalize, Validate, Audit
description: Don't dump everything into one giant document. To avoid DocumentDB's 16MB BSON limit and the unbounded-array anti-pattern (one expert's edit locking a whole brand), use four collections: seo_contexts (same-lifecycle text/URL/query embedded), seo_link_box_clusters (referencing contexts by numeric id to avoid duplication), seo_config_metadata (the single version pointer), and seo_audit_log.

Add the validation that code-embedded config never could: schema/syntax checks on the write path so a malformed parameter is rejected at entry instead of crashing the daily batch. Required indexes back the access patterns — {brandId,status} for the batch read, {brandId,contextId} unique for Toolbox lookups.

Capture the audit trail as a best-effort side-write: update the context first, then insert an audit record of { field, before, after }. If the audit insert fails, log/alert to Datadog but still return 200 — the primary write is preserved. A TTL index purges audit records older than ~90 days. Cluster referential integrity is 'tolerate silently': archived context ids are filtered at read time rather than blocking the archive.

view.caption: One API write touches the context + audit collections; audit failure alerts but never fails the user.

steps.decisionPrompt: How do you model this so a single edit can't lock a whole brand or blow the 16MB limit — and how do you record audit without transactions?

concepts.definition: Embedding an ever-growing list inside one document so every write rewrites/locks the whole document.
concepts.whyItMatters: It serializes unrelated edits and risks the 16MB document ceiling.
concepts.example: All of a brand's contexts in one array → two experts editing different contexts contend.

concepts.definition: Commit the primary write; attempt the secondary write; on failure alert but don't roll back.
concepts.whyItMatters: Avoids multi-document transaction cost while keeping the valuable-but-not-critical record.
concepts.example: Context saved → audit insert fails → CloudWatch alert, user still gets 200.

whyNow: With concurrency safe, the document shape itself becomes the next correctness/perf risk.

traps.trap: Embedding clusters inside every context document.
traps.why: Clusters reference many contexts; embedding duplicates them massively and bloats documents.
traps.instead: Separate collection referencing contexts by numeric id.

traps.trap: Wrapping context + audit writes in a multi-document transaction.
traps.why: Adds latency and code complexity for a record that's valuable but not request-critical.
traps.instead: Best-effort audit insert; if stronger guarantees are needed later, route audit via SQS with retries.

recap.before: One-document-fits-all with no validation or history.
recap.after: Four purpose-built collections, write-path validation, and a best-effort audit trail.
recap.newRisk: The new store must provably produce the exact same payload as the code before we trust it.

steps.title: 6. Migrate Safely: Shadow Mode, Parity, Cutover
description: Prove parity before trusting DocumentDB. Seed the collections offline (extract embedded CSVs/hardcoded values, transform to JSON, bulk-upsert via a Bastion host; create indexes after the bulk load, not before). Run a Final Delta Sync just before shadow mode to catch any code-level hotfixes made after seeding.

In SeoConfigSource: shadow, the batch loads via BOTH the code loader and the DocumentDB loader and a SeoConfigComparer structurally diffs the resulting C# dictionaries. The comparer must use set-equality (order-insensitive) on list fields like DistributionTypes/EstateTypes — CSV parse order vs BSON deserialization order differ and would otherwise produce false positives. Shadow always prioritizes the code output for stability.

Success criterion: 0 discrepancy logs for 7 continuous days, with Lead Engineer + Engineering Manager sign-off, then flip the flag to docdb. Any discrepancy → fix the seed, reset the 7-day counter, re-run. Preserve the Immonet quirk (TextConfig/SearchQueries must be null, not empty) on both seed and load.

view.caption: Shadow mode: code loader (baseline) and DocumentDB loader (candidate) feed a comparer; discrepancies log to Datadog.

steps.decisionPrompt: How do you guarantee the new store yields a byte-equivalent payload before cutover — and roll back if not?

concepts.definition: Run the new path alongside the authoritative old path, comparing outputs without serving the new one.
concepts.whyItMatters: De-risks a data-source swap by proving equivalence on real data before cutover.
concepts.example: Code loader vs DocumentDB loader, compared every batch run for 7 days.

concepts.definition: Compare collections by membership, ignoring element order.
concepts.whyItMatters: Different serializers emit list elements in different orders; strict order checks false-positive.

whyNow: A reversible, proven cutover is the last gate before the new write path is authoritative.

failureDrills.scenario: Comparer logs a discrepancy on day 4.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Batch keeps serving the code output (shadow prioritizes code).
failureDrills.mitigation: Triage the log, fix the seed/data, reset the 7-day counter, re-run shadow.

failureDrills.expectedBehavior: DB now has newer data than the cache (eventual consistency); Datadog APM alerts.
failureDrills.mitigation: ECS retry or manual re-trigger; the live cache keeps serving the last good payload.

traps.trap: Comparing list fields with strict array equality.
traps.why: CSV parse order and BSON deserialization order differ — guaranteed false positives.
traps.instead: Use set-equality (order-insensitive) for IList<string> fields in the comparer.

traps.trap: Creating indexes before the bulk upsert.
traps.why: Index maintenance during bulk insert dramatically slows seeding.

recap.after: 7 days of zero discrepancies → flag flipped to docdb; reversible throughout.
recap.newRisk: A stateful production DB now needs hardening — network isolation, encryption, secrets, recovery.

steps.title: 7. Harden It: Security, Access, Recovery
description: Introducing a stateful production database raises the security bar. Network isolation: the DocumentDB cluster lives only in private subnets with no public endpoint. Zero direct developer access from local machines — all seeding/admin runs via an EC2 Bastion / AWS Session Manager / CI runners inside the VPC.

Encryption: AES-256 at rest via KMS (storage, backups, snapshots); TLS 1.2+ in transit, with the .NET MongoClientSettings explicitly trusting the AWS RDS CA bundle. Credentials live in AWS Secrets Manager, rotated every 30 days with the dual-secret pattern for zero-downtime rotation (DocumentDB has no native IAM auth, so ECS tasks get read-only Secrets Manager access). Configure maxPoolSize (~50, tune by load test) so the driver doesn't exhaust DocumentDB's per-instance connection limit.

Human Toolbox access is governed by the corporate IdP with RBAC. Disaster recovery: point-in-time recovery with a 35-day window means an accidental mass UI delete can be rolled back to the second. Every document keeps lastModifiedBy/At/version for lineage.

view.caption: Hardened write path: RBAC via IdP, rotating secrets, private-subnet DB, observability.

steps.decisionPrompt: What new attack surface and operational risk does a stateful prod DB add, and how do you contain each?

concepts.definition: Keep two valid credential versions during rotation so in-flight connections never break.
concepts.whyItMatters: Enables zero-downtime 30-day credential rotation without coordinated restarts.
concepts.example: Secrets Manager rotates DocumentDB creds; both old and new are accepted during the window.

concepts.definition: Restore the database to any second within a retention window.
concepts.whyItMatters: A mass accidental UI delete becomes recoverable instead of catastrophic.
concepts.example: 35-day PITR window; roll back to the second before the bad bulk delete.

whyNow: Security and recoverability are the entry price for running a stateful store the business depends on.

bottlenecks.issue: Driver defaults exhaust DocumentDB's per-instance connection limit under load.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Explicitly set MongoClientSettings.maxPoolSize (~50) and tune from load tests.

failureDrills.scenario: Mass accidental deletion via the Toolbox UI.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Live cache keeps serving (DB not on hot path); data is recoverable.
failureDrills.mitigation: Point-in-time recovery to the second before the deletion (35-day window).

recap.before: A new prod DB with no guardrails.
recap.after: Private-subnet, encrypted, RBAC-gated, rotation-secured, PITR-recoverable store.
recap.newRisk: Operating cost and the accepted 24h propagation SLA — revisit force-refresh in v2.

finalDesign.title: Target Final Design — SEO Toolbox on DocumentDB
finalDesign.description: SEO experts edit through the Toolbox UI (RBAC via the corporate IdP). The Toolbox API validates input, enforces optimistic concurrency control on a version field, writes to AWS DocumentDB across four purpose-built collections, and records a best-effort audit trail. The daily batch reads all published documents from DocumentDB, enriches them, builds the payload, and bumps a single version pointer. The API's background refresher polls that pointer every 5 minutes and atomically swaps the in-memory FrozenDictionary snapshot — so the millions of client read requests stay zero-I/O O(1) lookups and DocumentDB is never on the hot path. The whole migration was proven via shadow-mode parity before a reversible feature-flag cutover.

functional.requirement: Self-service config edits without a deploy
functional.how: Toolbox UI/API writing to DocumentDB replaces code-embedded config as the SSoT.
Normalized collections: contexts embed same-lifecycle data; clusters reference contexts by id.

functional.requirement: Validate input on the write path
functional.how: Schema/syntax validation in the Toolbox API rejects bad parameters at entry.
Best-effort audit-log inserts capturing field-level before/after, with TTL retention.

functional.requirement: Live app keeps serving compiled config
functional.how: Daily batch + refresher feed the same in-memory snapshot as before.

nonFunctional.how: DocumentDB is never on the hot path; reads stay on the FrozenDictionary snapshot.
OCC: version-conditional update returns 409 on conflict.
Shadow mode requires 7 days of zero discrepancies behind a feature flag.
Private subnets, KMS/TLS encryption, rotating secrets, RBAC, 35-day PITR.

tradeoff: DocumentDB: flexible nested docs + ad-hoc querying for an evolving admin UI, but extra infra cost.
DynamoDB: cheaper/scalable and conditional writes give clean OCC, but upfront access-pattern rigidity hurts a new UI.
PostgreSQL JSONB: avoids JOINs but treating relational as a document store fights the POCO models.
OCC is contention-free and cheap at low write volume; pessimistic locking would be overkill here.
Polling a version pointer keeps the DB off the hot path; the cost is up-to-5-min staleness on top of the 24h batch SLA.
Managed rotation (dual-secret, 30-day) buys zero-downtime credential rotation without bespoke tooling.
Datadog APM + CloudWatch surface shadow-mode discrepancies, audit-write failures, and batch failures.

say: This is a write-path migration, not a greenfield build — the read path is already optimal and must not change.
State the governing rule up front: redefine the write path, protect the read path at all costs.
Pick DocumentDB for flexible nested docs and ad-hoc querying on an evolving admin UI.
Be honest that DynamoDB would also work at this tiny write volume and its conditional writes are clean OCC — so OCC is not the deciding factor; query flexibility is.
Version-pointer polling + atomic snapshot swap keeps DocumentDB off the hot path.
Only the batch bumps the version, preserving the 24h SLA.
OCC with a version field and 409 on conflict.
Four normalized collections; best-effort audit; shadow-mode parity with set-equality before a reversible cutover.
Private subnets, KMS/TLS, rotating secrets, RBAC, 35-day PITR.
Call out the accepted 24h propagation SLA and the v2 force-refresh idea.

expectations: Recognizes config-in-code blocks self-service and proposes a database + UI.
Knows a cache sits in front of reads and the DB shouldn't be hit per request.
Articulates CQRS-lite and keeps the DB off the hot path via a refresher/version pointer.
Implements OCC with a version field and 409 conflicts.
Models more than one collection to avoid giant documents.
Designs the version-pointer + atomic-swap invalidation and explains why only the batch bumps it.
Plans shadow-mode parity (set-equality), delta sync, and a reversible feature-flag cutover.
Pushes back on the DocumentDB-vs-DynamoDB reasoning honestly (OCC isn't a differentiator at this volume).
Treats it as risk management: parity proof, rollback, brand-specific quirks (Immonet nulls), connection-pool limits.
Names the missing capacity estimates that should gate instance sizing, and the accepted SLA/cost tradeoffs.
Designs security/recovery (private subnets, KMS, dual-secret rotation, RBAC, PITR) as first-class, not an afterthought.

followUps: What document count, published-payload size, and batch read throughput justify the chosen DocumentDB instance class? (The proposal sizes the cluster without these.)
If OCC isn't a true DocumentDB differentiator, what would actually change your mind toward DynamoDB or PostgreSQL JSONB?
How would a v2 'force refresh' for urgent fixes work without breaking the 24h-SLA protection of the read path?
How do you handle a context archived while still referenced by a cluster beyond 'tolerate silently'?
If best-effort audit becomes insufficient, how exactly would the SQS-backed audit path work (ordering, retries, idempotency)?
How do you keep the Immonet null-vs-empty quirk from regressing as the schema evolves?
